updates.q: MERGE / UPSERT pattern for idempotent loads?
updates.a: MERGE INTO target USING source ON keys WHEN MATCHED UPDATE WHEN NOT MATCHED INSERT. Postgres uses INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (key) DO UPDATE. Combined with a stable natural key, re-runs converge to the same state.

new_cards.q: RANK vs DENSE_RANK vs ROW_NUMBER on a tie?
new_cards.a: Two rows tied at position 2: ROW_NUMBER gives 1,2,3,4 (arbitrary tiebreak). RANK gives 1,2,2,4 (skips 3). DENSE_RANK gives 1,2,2,3 (no gap).

new_cards.q: NTILE(4) does what?
new_cards.a: Divides the ordered partition into 4 roughly equal buckets and labels each row 1..4. Standard tool for quartiles, deciles (NTILE(10)), or percentile bands.

new_cards.q: Window frame: ROWS vs RANGE?
new_cards.a: ROWS BETWEEN N PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW counts physical rows. RANGE counts logical values of the ORDER BY column (ties grouped). Default is RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING TO CURRENT ROW -- subtle bug source on duplicate timestamps.

new_cards.q: Deduplicate rows keeping the latest version?
new_cards.a: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY natural_key ORDER BY updated_at DESC) AS rn, then WHERE rn = 1 in an outer query/CTE. Works for CDC streams and replayed events.

new_cards.q: Antijoin: rows in A with no match in B?
new_cards.a: LEFT JOIN B ON ... WHERE B.id IS NULL, or NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM B WHERE ...). NOT EXISTS is NULL-safe; NOT IN breaks on any NULL in B's column.

new_cards.q: Why does a LEFT JOIN inflate row counts?
new_cards.a: The right table has multiple matching rows per left key, so each left row fans out. Fix: pre-aggregate the right side in a CTE, or join on a unique key, or use DISTINCT ON / window-then-filter.

new_cards.q: CTE vs subquery vs temp table?
new_cards.a: Subquery / CTE = inline, planner usually inlines them (Postgres 12+ inlines non-recursive CTEs). Temp table = materialized, stats collected, reused across queries. Use temp tables when the intermediate is large and reused.

new_cards.q: B-tree vs hash vs GIN vs BRIN -- when to pick each?
new_cards.a: B-tree: equality + range, default. Hash: equality only, rarely better than B-tree. GIN: multi-value columns (arrays, jsonb, full-text). BRIN: huge tables with physical-order correlation (time-series append-only), tiny footprint.

new_cards.q: Index selectivity -- why does it matter?
new_cards.a: Selectivity = distinct values / rows. Low-selectivity columns (e.g. boolean is_active) make indexes useless because the planner correctly chooses a seq scan. Index high-cardinality columns or use partial/covering indexes.

new_cards.q: EXPLAIN vs EXPLAIN ANALYZE?
new_cards.a: EXPLAIN shows the planner's estimated plan + costs without executing. EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually runs the query and prints real row counts and timings -- the only way to spot estimate-vs-actual skew that drives bad plans.

new_cards.q: N+1 query problem?
new_cards.a: Code fetches a list of N parents, then issues one child query per parent (N+1 total round trips). Fix: a single JOIN, an IN (...) batch fetch, or ORM eager loading (e.g. SQLAlchemy selectinload).

new_cards.q: GROUP BY vs HAVING vs WHERE?
new_cards.a: WHERE filters rows before grouping. GROUP BY collapses rows by key. HAVING filters groups after aggregation. You cannot reference an aggregate in WHERE, only HAVING.

new_cards.q: ROLLUP vs CUBE vs GROUPING SETS?
new_cards.a: ROLLUP (a, b, c): hierarchical subtotals + grand total. CUBE: every subset (2^n combinations). GROUPING SETS: explicit list of grouping combos. All emit one result set in a single pass instead of UNION ALL.

new_cards.q: Columnar storage -- why fast for analytics?
new_cards.a: Each column stored contiguously, so SELECT SUM(amount) reads only that column's pages. Adjacent values compress well (RLE, dictionary). Row stores must read every column of every row -- huge IO waste for wide tables.

new_cards.q: Partitioning vs sharding?
new_cards.a: Partitioning: one DB splits a table into segments by key (date, hash) for prune-on-scan; same node. Sharding: data split across multiple nodes/databases for horizontal scale. Partition first, shard only when one node can't hold it.

new_cards.q: Transactional outbox pattern?
new_cards.a: Inside the same DB transaction, write the business row AND an outbox row. A separate process tails the outbox (or via CDC) and publishes to Kafka. Guarantees the event ships iff the transaction commits -- no dual-write race.

new_cards.q: Lambda vs Kappa architecture?
new_cards.a: Lambda: parallel batch + speed (streaming) layers, merged at query time -- duplicates logic. Kappa: a single streaming pipeline; reprocess history by replaying the log. Modern stacks (Flink, Materialize) favor Kappa.

new_cards.q: dbt model layering convention?
new_cards.a: staging (1:1 cleanup of source tables) -> intermediate (joins, business logic) -> marts (fact + dim tables consumed by BI). Tests and docs live alongside models; refs build the DAG.

new_cards.q: SCD Type 1 vs 2 vs 4?
new_cards.a: Type 1: overwrite -- no history. Type 2: new row per change with valid_from/valid_to -- full history (default for analytics). Type 4: keep current row in main table + a separate history table for changes.

new_cards.q: Snowflake vs BigQuery vs Redshift -- 2026 tradeoff?
new_cards.a: Snowflake: separated storage/compute, per-warehouse scaling, multi-cloud, strong governance. BigQuery: serverless, slot-based pricing, deepest GCP + ML integration. Redshift: AWS-native, RA3 separates storage, cheapest at steady high utilization but more tuning.
